title: 2017-18 & 18-19 LVN-RN Comprehensive Program Review Two year cycle (Part 1)
text:
Comprehensive Program Review Two-Year Cycle
LVN-RN Step Up Program
Rhonda Brown, Assistant Director, Faculty
Hallie Coppi, Faculty
Kim Parham, Faculty
Cora Brownell, Director
December 2018
I. Description of the Program
The LVN-RN Step-up Program is designed as an accelerated course for the LVN with a license in
good standing to earn an Associate’s Degree in Nursing and be eligible to sit for the National RN
exam.
The conceptual framework is based on the nursing process. The nursing process allows for and
supports application and utilization of knowledge from the biological, natural and social sciences
with appreciation and respect for individuals, families, and communities from all cultural,
religious, and social backgrounds. It provides a framework for systematic nursing practice in a
variety of settings for a diverse patient population across the life span.
The nursing program courses are sequential and build on previously acquired knowledge and skills.
Overriding all student experiences, the ability to think critically is emphasized. Critical thinking is
imperative in all aspects of client care; providing a safe patient environment, priority setting,
determining appropriate interventions based on scope of practice, and delegating.
Within the curriculum conceptual framework of the nursing process, four concepts provide
additional foundational underpinnings; pathophysiology, growth and development, pharmacology,
and QSEN (knowledge, skills, and attitudes in the 6 domains of patient centered care, teamwork
and collaboration, evidence- based practice, quality improvement, safety, and informatics). As
content areas are discussed, the four concepts are integrated.
Finally, threads, or topics in nursing integral to nursing knowledge and practice, are leveled in the
courses. The 7 threads are nutrition, legal/ethical, cultural awareness, teaching/learning, advocacy,
leadership/professionalism, and communication.

b) Primary purpose
Graduate a competent entry-level graduate-RN nurse qualified to pass the NCLEX
licensing exam and practice in a safe and effective novice role.
The faculty believe that nursing is both a profession and a discipline guided by professional
standards and ethics that is responsive to the changing health needs of patients, families,
and communities. Nursing promotes, maintains, or restores wellness and prevents disease.
Nursing faculty also believe nursing is both an art and a science and requires critical
thinking, synthesizing knowledge while providing competency-based skills with
proficiency. Further, the nursing faculty endorse the 6 domains of QSEN and believe the
principles should inform nursing education with particular attention to patient centered care,
safety, and collaboration.
The faculty, acknowledging the dynamic nature of nursing, is committed to integrating
evidenced based concepts and practices into the educational process and instilling the
importance of currency, lifelong learning, and collaborative, patient centered care to
achieve positive patient outcomes.
Students assume primary responsibility for their own learning while faculty provides
educational opportunities for knowledge acquisition and professional role development.
The student is considered and expected to be an active participant in the educational
experience.
The faculty view each student as a unique individual with special abilities, talents, needs,
and goals. Cultural diversity, varying life experiences, and changing socioeconomic factors
affect each student differently. The faculty function as role model, coach, motivator,
facilitator, and resources in the learning process by providing direction for learning and
growth as a health care provider. Adult learning principles are the basis for teaching.
c) Whom we serve include demographics
The program serves the local community of Siskiyou County, neighboring counties of
Shasta, and Lassen. Additionally, Jackson and Josephine, Oregon counties utilize our

d) What kind of services we provide:
• Pre-Admission Testing- Test of Essential Academic Skills (TEAS) required for
application to the program.
• Algebra and APA writing skills remediation.
• California Board of Registered Nursing (BRN) approved curriculum. The
curriculum includes theory/ clinical/ skills lab/ simulation/remediation in core
nursing content. Core nursing content includes Medical/Surgical,
Community/Mental Health, and Maternal/Child Nursing care.
• Assessment Technologies Institute (ATI), a National Council Licensure
Examination (NCLEX) vendor that provides NCLEX preparedness tutorials, quiz
banks, practice tests, and proctored tests in all areas of NCLEX content areas of
testing.
• Degrees Awarded

f) Curriculum Current and needs based:
Curriculum revision for all courses was completed and approved by Board of
Registered Nursing May 2017. Implemented Fall 2018. New curriculum incorporated
contemporary nursing education concepts as set forth by industry educational,
accreditation, and national testing bodies.
AS-T articulation agreement with CSU Chico for BSN. See plan below.

Maximum of 70 units may be transferred to Chico State.
20 transferrable Nursing units are needed over and above the 70 units transferred to Chico State and
will be used as 20 units of NCLEX credit, issued in the final semester of the RN to BSN Program.
The remaining 8.5-16 transferrable Nursing units can count towards the 70 units transferred in to
CSUC
The following COS Nursing units are transferrable: NURS 1045 Medical Terminology (3), NURS 1047
Career Pathways in Health I (2), and NURS 1048 Career Pathways in Health II (1.5) .
*Note: Some LVN Programs (such as Butte College) have transferrable to CSU nursing units – look at
the catalog where your LVN courses were taken – if they are transferrable, up to a total of 16 nursing
units can be used in transfer and the units needed on page 1 would be reduced to 54.

Prerequisites:
1. Must be eligible for admission to the University.
2. Eligibility for admission to the RN to BSN Nursing major:
• Graduation from an associate degree program in nursing (can be in final semester).
• 54 transferrable non-nursing units (GE certification courses, Nursing prerequisites & co-requisites).
• Completion of CSU, Chico lower division GE requirements.
• Completion of all CSU, Chico Nursing prerequisite courses (Anatomy, Physiology, Microbiology, Chemistry,
Oral Communication, Written Communication, Critical Thinking, & Statistics) with minimum “C” grade.
• Cumulative GPA of 3.0 (will accept most recent 60 units); 3.0 in Nursing is required to apply before
graduation
• Must be accepted into the RN to BSN Program by the School of Nursing before applying to CSU, Chico.

II. External Factors with Significant Impact
a) Budgetary constraints or opportunities: The Enrollment Growth Grant provides funding
for purchase and maintenance of Simulation and Skills Lab equipment and software for smart
mannequins and
control room, disposable practice supplies for students, subscription fees for DocuCare, an
electronic health care record system, salaries, continuing education expenses for faculty and
staff.
b) Competition from other institutions: None. We are one of seven statewide LVN-RN Step
Up programs.
c) Requirements for four -year institutions: ADN degree and RN license in good standing.
Chico State University transfer agreement, see complete pathway above. Many on-line
universities will accept our degree in conjunction with an RN license in good standing such as
Western Governors University.

title: 2017-18 & 18-19 LVN-RN Comprehensive Program Review Two year cycle (Part 7)
text:
d) Institutional regulation, policies, standards, and other mandates:
• BRN faculty minimum requirements impact our ability to hire qualified instructors. The
frontier county of Siskiyou has a shortage of BSN and MSN prepared nurses. Many of
our instructors live in Shasta county. This is helpful for Shasta county clinical sites.
• Faculty Association and Administrative policies regarding non-instructional time. The
BRN mandates new faculty orientation to clinical sites and clinical expert time for
curriculum review. The issue of compensation for this required time remains in
negotiations at the level of the Faculty Association and Administration.
It’s difficult to recruit and retain qualified nurses working in industry due to lack of
compensated time for orientation.
• Clinical sites located as far as Sacramento, CA. In order to meet BRN requirements for
clinical rigor, our students and faculty have to drive great distances to meet this
requirement.
• The overall salary compensation column model does not reflect the continuing
education requirements of the BRN and Nursing Specialty Certification Boards. For
example, this writers AACN Psychiatric-Mental Health Nurse Board Certification
Credential requires 100 continuing education hours (CE) every 5 years. Any content
expert in the nursing program has an advanced credential to qualify. The program
requires content experts in all five areas of core instruction. The BRN mandates 30
CE’s every two years. An equivalency for this mandated education and additional
college courses per the step process would be equitable.
e) Non-Institutional regulation, policies, standards, and other mandates:
• Clinical facility sites impact students/ faculty with varying policies and mandates. This
requires extra time to meet, verify, and track all applicable policies and mandates.
• Clinical site partners do not charge fees for our students practicing in their facilities.
f) Job market
• Requirements of prospective employers: ADN degree and RN license in good
standing. BSN preferred. Preceptorship or work experience preferred.
• Development in field: Concurrent industry goal of 80% of RN’s in workforce have
BSN degree by 2020. Current benchmark not on target nationally. The goal will remain
in effect with the goal end date extended. The highest regarded obstacle is a lack of
Mastered prepared Nurse Educators to meet enrollment needs.
III. Progress on Outcomes Assessment:
Program Outcomes
a) Program Outcomes:
RN Program Outcomes
At completion of the program the student will:
1. Demonstrates a broad base of nursing and supportive (physical, psychological, social, spiritual)
knowledge and critical thinking while providing culturally aware, evidenced based, safe,
patient centered care to a diverse patient population across the life span.
Outcome met as evidenced by program completion and NCLEX pass rate.

-- 10 of 19 --

title: 2017-18 & 18-19 LVN-RN Comprehensive Program Review Two year cycle (Part 8)
text:
2. Utilize critical thinking and the nursing process in developing and implementing evidence-
based patient plan of care, including teaching, to provide safe, quality, and comprehensive
nursing care.
Outcome met as evidenced by program completion and NCLEX pass rate.
3. Demonstrate proficiency and competency when performing nursing skills and procedures,
integrating QSEN concepts, to achieve positive patient outcomes.
Outcome met as evidenced by program completion and NCLEX pass rate.
4. Demonstrate and provide care consistent with scope of practice, legal, ethical and advocacy
principles, and regulatory and organizational requirement to achieve positive patient outcomes.
Outcome met as evidenced by program completion and NCLEX pass rate.
5. Demonstrate communication and technology skills essential for providing and documenting
safe patient care.
Outcome met as evidenced by program completion and NCLEX pass rate.
6. Function effectively as a team member/leader incorporating knowledge of healthcare team
member roles and participate collaboratively, fostering open communication, mutual respect,
and shared decision-making to achieve quality patient care and positive patient outcomes.
Outcome met as evidenced by program completion and NCLEX pass rate.
7. Demonstrates professionalism, leadership skills, integrity, and accountability in nursing
practice, and verbalize understanding of importance of lifelong learning to maintain standards
of competent performance.
Outcome met as evidenced by program completion and NCLEX pass rate.

title: 2017-18 & 18-19 LVN-RN Comprehensive Program Review Two year cycle (Part 12)
text:
III. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
SEE GRAPH UNDER I(g).
c.) FT/PT Faculty Ratio/Impact on Student Success: Due to the compressed calendar, the
program has had to condense lecture material, thus forcing more self-responsibility on the student
for class preparation and study. Typically, the first semester is a challenge for students taking two
separate nursing courses concurrently. The LVN course involves one intensive course each
semester. The part time clinical faculty are not always committed to review the theory and relate it
to clinical time. This may impact possible remediation opportunities.
The data shows the student/faculty ratios are adequate for success.
IV. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative/Qualitative Research
a) Transfers, degrees, certificates, satisfaction, student contacts, Perkins data, equity data
During the review period of 2015 to 2018, 112 Associate in Science degrees were awarded to
graduates of the LVN to RN Step-up Program:
LVN-RN Step-up - Associate in Science 112
2015 29
2016 28
2017 30
2018 25
Equity information and data is addressed in Section I c) of this review. Demographics are not
reflective of the county population. Few minorities are represented in the LVN to RN Nursing
Program. Students with English as a second language enroll, but the success rate for them is 50
to 75%, depending upon the academic year. English as a second language remains one of the
most prominent factors leading to unsuccessful outcome for any student.
Contacts with students are frequent and consistent throughout the students’ application and
participation in the program. About 6 months after graduation, students receive a 28 question
survey which asks input on specifics regarding employment, preparation for employment, and
input on the preparation that the program provided for NCLEX and employment. At around
the same period of time, COS Nursing solicits input from employers of program graduates on
the preparation and performance of the graduates.
A Road Map to CSU Chico offers opportunity for COS LVN to RN graduates to obtain a BSN
in approximately 14 months. Every late fall to early spring, the Nursing Program hosts an
information session for the students with the CSU Chico Nursing Department Liaison, who
explains the process and offers the students support in the enrollment into the CSU program.
CSU reserves slots for graduates of Far North regional ADN programs, and some of those slots
are not always filled.
The most prevalently used measure for successful outcomes in Nursing pre-licensure education
is the NCLEX pass rate. While students are encouraged to take the NCLEX as soon as possible
after graduation, some graduates delay testing. As a result, annual pass rates are not
consistently reflective of success within an academic year. COS Nursing NCLEX-RN pass
rates for this review period are: 97.62, 95.83, 93.1, and 89.41 percent for the 4-year review
period. The LVN to RN program received the Chancellor’s Office Gold Star award for high
rates of employment and NCLEX performance in 2017 year. The admission process for the

-- 16 of 19 --

title: 2017-18 & 18-19 LVN-RN Comprehensive Program Review Two year cycle (Part 13)
text:
program has ended up with a wait list ranked by points. As the list grew, cohorts became more
homogenous with less variation in attributes. The cohorts for the last two years of this review
period tended to be filled with applicants ranking lower on the admission rubric scale.
b) Summary of results of these measures
The retention rate of students enrolled is consistently high, and overall success rate is also high
as reflected by NCLEX and employment data. This reflects upon the diligence in admissions,
instruction, and student performance. Overall, the program is quite successful in these areas.
The lack of minority representation and the lower success of ESL students require further
attention, and focus. Although advancement is offered for advancement to the BSN, this
opportunity is not well taken advantage of by COS graduates. Most graduates do not
immediately transfer to a BSN program. The ranked wait list now has too many applicants to
allow for cohorts with a mix of attributes, leading to cohorts that are consistently lower in
academic proficiency with no more proficient classmates to infuse the group with incentive and
tactics for improvement. The last two cohorts were more homogenous in the lower ranking
students, and NCLEX pass rates declined during the last two years.
c) What did you learn from the evaluation of these measures, what improvements have you
implemented or plan to implement?
At this time, COS Nursing is participating on a statewide level with other pre-licensure nursing
programs to find best solutions within the constantly changing world of Nursing Education.
The imperative to produce more BSN level graduates has driven a current project to develop
concurrent ADN and BSN programs patterned off of some currently developed and used in
other states such as Arizona and Kansas. As of the admissions for 2019-20 cohort, the waitlist
will be discontinued, and applicants that are not admitted can reapply and gain points for higher
ranking on their subsequent applications. The process is expected to focus on applicants with a
high potential for success while mixed with those with those that ranked lower. This process is
expected to be inclusive as it matures through the next several years. While higher ranking
applicants do well in academic components, many lower ranking applicants appear to have
more developed inter-personal and reflective skills in the direct care setting. The mix of
abilities is the goal for the most successful cohort, which providing opportunity for all who
meet the qualifying standards.
V. Evaluation
a) Representativeness of population served: See I (e), reviews in section IV. In summary, the
enrollment in the COS LVN-RN Program does not represent the population served. Outreach
to threshold minority populations of Hispanic, African American, and Native American
prospective nursing students is needed.
b) Alternative modes and schedules of delivery: Canvas augmented classes, Electronic medical
record system, Hi fidelity simulation lab
c) Partnerships COMMUNITY CLINICAL SITES; RestPadd-Red Bluff, Shasta Regional
Medical Center, Vibra Hospital, Fairchild Medical Center, Dignity-Mt. Shasta, Madrone
Hospice, Mercy Hospice, Siskiyou County Behavioral Health, Siskiyou County Jail, Siskiyou
County Public Health, Siskiyou County Department of Education, Siskiyou Home Health, Fall
River Hospital, Shriners Hospital.
d) Innovation and Implementation of best practices: Full fidelity manikins for medical surgical
and obstetrics clinical simulations. Current faculty meet the standards of BRN required content
experts, who infuse the instruction and curriculum with updates and latest practices.

title: 2017-18 & 18-19 LVN-RN Comprehensive Program Review Two year cycle (Part 14)
text:
-- 17 of 19 --

e) Efficiency in operations: Reduction of costs is currently a high priority. One effort is the
piloting of moving NURS 1021from summer to spring session (2019) in order to decrease
salary costs for nursing budget. Historically the course was taught in the summer by faculty at
the adjunct rate. Three of the four full time faculty members have a MSN and can teach across
all curriculums, leading to cost-effective flexibility in assignements.
f) Efficiency in resource use: The use of Canvas has decreased the use of paper for student
assignments, schedules, and other items previously copied. We continue to evaluate resource
allocation whether it be staffing or supplies to improve the nursing budget.

Participation in shared governance:
h) Professional development and training: All full-time faculty meet Flex obligations.
Nursing Director provides current educational in-house in services and trainings for all faculty
development. Faculty are afforded additional out of town trainings on such topics as NCLEX
testing, Conceptual Curriculum development.
i) Compliance with applicable mandates: All faculty comply with BRN licensing CPR,
and immunization requirements as specified by the college and the clinical agencies under contract
to host student clinical experience and instruction.
VI. Vision
Continue to offer an excellent ADN nursing program to meet the needs of
our community and partners.
VII. Progress on Prior Goals: The new LVN-RN Step-Up Program curriculum was launched
in 2017-18. The program was re-evaluated in summer 2018 and appropriate changes were made to
meet the need of the students and to keep current with trends and evidenced-based best practice
standards.
VIII. Two-year Action Plan:
• Continue to deliver a quality rigorous academic and clinical nursing program to produce
excellent new RN graduate nurses.
• Educate all faculty on the Next Gen NCLEX testing plan and adapt curriculum and teaching
delivery methods to deliver a minimum 90% pass rate on the updated testing plan.
• Continue to evaluate students’ needs and nursing budget dollar allocation for continued student
success.
We the undersigned certify that the LVN-RN Step-Up Program, program review process included
broad dialogue about student learning, program effectiveness and resource allocation.
Rhonda Brown Principal Preparer
Cora Brownell Contributor
